question_title: Humans naturally categorize the world using hierarchical concepts (animal → dog → husky), while trad...
answer_text: Humans naturally categorize the world using hierarchical concepts (animal → dog → husky), while traditional neural networks mainly learn pixel patterns and fine details without understanding broader meanings. AliGNet from Google DeepMind aims to bridge this gap by aligning machine representations with human conceptual structures. The idea relies on representation learning, where neural networks convert images into embeddings (abstract feature vectors). When these features are properly disentangled (e.g., separating color, shape, lighting), the model can generalize better and understand objects by meaning rather than appearance. Interestingly, this aligns with ideas from cognitive science and even Plato’s theory of Forms, which argued that real knowledge comes from understanding abstract, stable concepts behind physical examples. Modern AI research is essentially trying to build systems that capture these invariant representations. #AI #DeepLearning #AliGNet #RepresentationLearning #MachineLearning #CognitiveScience #ArtificialIntelligence #ConceptLearning #Embeddings #Disentanglement #HumanAIAlignment #Plato #PhilosophyOfAI #AIResearch #FutureOfAI

answer_text: The article responds to Ethan Mollick's recent guide to AI tools, which is excellent and, for educators, quietly alarming. question_title: At a time when discussions about artificial intelligence in academia are everywhere, including debat...
answer_text: At a time when discussions about artificial intelligence in academia are everywhere, including debates about AI-assisted writing, academic integrity, and the future of research practices, an article based on the paper I presented at a conference in Cairo last year has now been published. The article examines several widely discussed cases in which AI text detection tools incorrectly labeled human-written texts as AI-generated, including studies showing that more than half of essays written by non-native English speakers were falsely flagged, cases where TOEFL essays were identified as AI-written, and even examples where detectors labeled the U.S. Constitution as AI-generated text. By looking at these examples, the article raises broader questions about how reliable such systems actually are when used in academic evaluation. Based on these observations, I remain skeptical about the growing reliance on AI detection tools in academic evaluation.

answer_text: A few weeks ago, I said the young adults gave me dirty looks when I tried to explain AI as a tool in class. Well, yesterday was a not-so-great day for academic integrity. But here’s the thing - copy/paste AI slop is rampant on social media. My students are learning that they can’t get away with it in class, but people all over this platform and every other platform seem to be leaning in to generative AI laziness. Young adults see this and believe it’s the answer for them, too. We have to stop this.

If you are my student, I’d rather you be authentically you every day of the week. Contact me if you’re struggling - go to your instructor, a human, not generative AI, which cites the wrong textbook (that happened.) You signed up for this class ideally to learn, so let’s try to actually make that happen.

Let’s bring humanity back to our communication and interaction.

question_title: What stands out in the latest reporting is not the novelty of ChatGPT, but the intentionality of tea...
answer_text: What stands out in the latest reporting is not the novelty of ChatGPT, but the intentionality of teachers who are reframing it as a partner in thinking, not a replacement for it. High school English teachers like Craig Schmidt in Illinois now explicitly require students to draft their own work first, then send it through an AI “writing partner” for critique, using structured worksheets that teach students how to specify a role (critic, teacher) and the type of feedback they want. The message is clear: the student owns the ideas; AI helps pressure-test the prose.

question_title: We are also seeing a shift from AI as a generic grammar checker to AI as a metacognitive mirror. Stu...
answer_text: We are also seeing a shift from AI as a generic grammar checker to AI as a metacognitive mirror. Students are learning to ask, “Where is my argument weak? How could my opening be more engaging?” and then to decide when to ignore AI’s advice. One student quoted in the piece described pushing back on a suggested rhetorical-question opening—exactly the kind of critical stance we want future writers to take with any tool, human or machine.

question_title: For educators and edtech leaders, this raises strategic questions: Do our policies still assume AI i...
answer_text: For educators and edtech leaders, this raises strategic questions: Do our policies still assume AI is an external threat, or are we designing guardrails that turn it into a scalable, individualized feedback layer for every writer in the building? Are we helping students build durable writing muscles by using AI after the first draft, to compare alternatives and reflect on choices, rather than outsourcing the blank page?

answer_text: If you lead a school, district, university, or an education company, I’d love to hear how you are approaching AI as a “writing partner” rather than a “writing engine.” What’s working, what’s worrying you, and where do you see the line between healthy coaching and over-dependence? Share your examples in the comments—or DM me if you’re interested in co-designing practical AI-in-writing guidelines for your classrooms.

question_title: Large Language Models (LLMs) are advanced artificial intelligence systems trained on massive amounts...
answer_text: Large Language Models (LLMs) are advanced artificial intelligence systems trained on massive amounts of text data to understand and generate human language. Using transformer-based neural networks, they can perform tasks such as answering questions, summarizing documents, translating languages, writing content, and generating computer code. LLMs learn patterns, grammar, and contextual relationships during training, enabling them to produce coherent and context-aware responses. They are widely used in education, business, healthcare, research, and creative industries.

question_title: Our education program of training in Artificial Intelligence and soft skills for the technology sect...
answer_text: Our education program of training in Artificial Intelligence and soft skills for the technology sector faced a challenge in manually grading 155 student Jupyter Notebooks for a Python AI education course. The initial approach of using a Large Language Model (LLM) for grading proved inefficient due to payload issues, AI hallucinations, and infinite loops. A re-architected multi-pass pipeline, incorporating semantic memory, LaTeX bypass, a sandboxed execution engine, and multi-tier LLM routing, significantly improved efficiency, reducing processing time from 50 minutes to seconds per student.

question_title: In this article, the authors explore how #large #language #models (#LLMs) can act as both patient tu...
answer_text: In this article, the authors explore how #large #language #models (#LLMs) can act as both patient tutors and collaborative partners to enhance education delivery. As tutors, LLMs personalize learning by offering step-by-step explanations and addressing individual needs, making education more inclusive for students with diverse backgrounds or abilities. As collaborators, they expand students’ horizons, supporting them in tackling complex, real-world problems and cocreating innovative projects. However, to fully realize these benefits, LLMs must be leveraged not as tools for providing direct solutions but rather to guide students in developing resolving strategies and finding learning paths together. Therefore, a strong emphasis should be placed on educating students and teachers on the successful use of LLMs to ensure their effective integration into classrooms. ---- Tosin Adewumi, Foteini Simistira Liwicki, Marcus Liwicki, Viktor Gardelli, Lama Alkhaled, Hamam Mokayed

question_title: The nursing profession currently navigates a dual crisis: a widening faculty shortage that limits in...
answer_text: The nursing profession currently navigates a dual crisis: a widening faculty shortage that limits instructional bandwidth and a clinical placement bottleneck that severely restricts student exposure to diverse patient populations and complex care environments. This article explores the evolution of "Nursing Education 2.0," a transformative era characterized by the integration of artificial intelligence (AI) and immersive virtual simulations. By examining hyper-personalized learning pathways, conversational virtual reality (VR), and automated skill assessments, this paper evaluates the efficacy of AI-driven pedagogy in preparing students for the modern healthcare landscape. Crucially, it addresses the burgeoning debate: Can AI virtual simulations replace hands-on clinical hours? The findings suggest that while AI provides a superior, psychologically safe environment for repetitive mastery and cognitive development, it serves as a sophisticated bridge rather than a total replacement for the human-centric nuances, sensory complexities, and unpredictable nature of bedside care.

question_title: This article examines two foundational diagnostic approaches, consistency-based diagnosis and abduct...
answer_text: This article examines two foundational diagnostic approaches, consistency-based diagnosis and abductive diagnosis, in the context of intelligent tutoring systems (ITS) for elementary physics education. Consistency-based diagnosis identifies errors by flagging discrepancies between student responses and a predefined model of correct behavior, offering simplicity and robustness even with incomplete data. Abductive diagnosis, on the other hand, infers the most plausible explanations for observed errors, leveraging causal rules and common misconceptions to deliver actionable feedback. Through a comparative analysis, this study explores the knowledge requirements and instructional value of each method. The findings suggest that while consistency-based diagnosis offers technical efficiency, abductive diagnosis is pedagogically superior due to its ability to mirror human reasoning, identify underlying misconceptions, and support personalized learning. Therefore, abductive diagnosis emerges as the more effective approach for intelligent tutoring systems aimed at enhancing conceptual understanding and targeted remediation in physics education.

question_title: In an era where AI is reshaping industries, the most valuable skill is not just technical expertise ...
answer_text: In an era where AI is reshaping industries, the most valuable skill is not just technical expertise but the ability to adapt and integrate technology into strategic decision-making. As algorithms become more sophisticated, the line between human intuition and machine precision blurs, demanding a new kind of leadership—one that leverages AI as a partner, not a replacement. The professionals who thrive will be those who master the art of asking the right questions, interpreting data with context, and aligning AI-driven insights with human-centric goals. True career growth lies in embracing lifelong learning, staying curious, and understanding that technology is a tool to amplify our potential, not define it. The future belongs to those who can bridge the gap between innovation and impact. #Inspiration #CareerGrowth #LifeLessons #ProfessionalDevelopment

question_title: Faculty keep telling me the same story: students paste readings into ChatGPT, show up with a summary...
answer_text: Faculty keep telling me the same story: students paste readings into ChatGPT, show up with a summary, and can hit the broad strokes. But push them on a specific passage or ask them to work through an argument, and the floor drops out. The instinct is to call this laziness. I don't think that's the whole picture.

question_title: For a meaningful number of these students, the issue isn't that they won't engage with the text. It'...
answer_text: For a meaningful number of these students, the issue isn't that they won't engage with the text. It's that they can't, at least not at the level the assignment demands. The literacy data bears this out: roughly a third of American eighth graders score below basic reading levels. These students never fully consolidated the intermediate literacy skills that make academic text a vehicle for learning rather than an obstacle to it. And when they reach for AI, it's not avoidance. It's a rational response to a real problem.

question_title: A tool called Dynamilis, for example, analyzes how a child writes on a tablet, not just the final pr...

question_title: But the paper is also candid about what we still don't know. Most studies have small samples, limite...
answer_text: But the paper is also candid about what we still don't know. Most studies have small samples, limited diversity, and lack longitudinal data. The authors write plainly: "This review confirms a gap in robust research on the effects of AI-based tools on students with SEN."

question_title: This Matt Shumer article is very interesting.  My attitude to AI for the most part has been that it'...
answer_text: This Matt Shumer article is very interesting.  My attitude to AI for the most part has been that it's great.  Great!  I use it all the time, but I haven't yet come to that moment described in the article where I see AI go from 'helpful tool' to 'does my job better than I do.' I teach English.  I produce materials for teaching English.  AI is very useful, but it doesn't create materials that I can use without editing them, and it's not just a matter of better prompts.  I might be tempted to think AI will never replace teachers, but if that is so, it won't be the materials production - eventually, AI will develop better instructional and assessment material than any human every could;  it would only be because there is something about the relationship between teacher and student that can't be replaced by AI, or at least the perception that this is true.  AI will probably replace teachers in fully online environments more quickly, but it may replace us in the physical classroom too unless learners really do prefer that their instructors are conscious, not merely intelligent, and are willing to pay for that feature.  Even then, what happens if it can be demonstrated that AI instruction, online or in a physical classroom, produces better outcomes for a fraction of the cost to the learner?  If learning language is a step toward a goal and not a social event or hobby, AI will be overtaking this career along with all the others.

answer_text: AI isn't going anywhere, and it's up to us to use it wisely and ethically, like any other tool. With every burst forward in technology, pedagogy is deeply impacted. Just think of how much the calculator has shaped the teaching of mathematics! As the tools we use become more sophisticated, like the move from using slide rules to the graphic calculator, how we learn and how we teach changes fundamentally. AI is a new tool, and while it can be abused, it can also be used in brilliant, time-saving ways. But the art of teaching - YES, I said ART - is one that cannot be adequately replicated through a purely mechanical process.

question_title: Epistemological access is about who gets to enter a discipline. Across many African institutions, ar...
answer_text: Epistemological access is about who gets to enter a discipline. Across many African institutions, arts education is constrained by limited instruments, studio space, software, or specialist training. AI helps level this field. A music student can hum a melody and explore harmony digitally without years of notation training. Visual art students can prototype complex ideas before accessing costly materials. Assistive AI tools also support learners with physical disabilities—ensuring creative vision is not constrained by circumstance.

question_title: Creativity is a process of iteration. AI accelerates this cycle. Design students can generate multip...
answer_text: Creativity is a process of iteration. AI accelerates this cycle. Design students can generate multiple solutions under defined constraints, while photography students experiment with lighting and narrative beyond budgetary limits. As a result, the student’s role shifts from execution to curation, judgment, and intentional choice. Foundational skills remain essential, but AI is introduced in stages to extend—not replace—learning.

question_title: While taking a break today between meeting suppliers and preparing new policies, I had a play with t...
answer_text: While taking a break today between meeting suppliers and preparing new policies, I had a play with the free music making function in Google Workspace for Education. It even has an edit via transcript button. I made a short song in an 80s hip hop style, about computational thinking. I reflected about who survives the evolution of AI in education, and what that means for us and our students. How many tools do we need? How many companies should we use? What does equitable learning look like? It's important to stop and reflect. The fact I made a quick music track on a learning concept is neat, but do we risk overwhelming ourselves and students with content over pedagogy? I like how Google is trying to build a coherent ecosystem to learn with AI. I think often about how we maintain independent thought and focused attention, in a world with so many digital opportunities to express ourselves. That's the point where I take stock, and imagine how my colleagues and their classes can leverage the best that's on offer, while remaining true to themselves. What do you think?

question_title: As I worked on an AI policy for my Spring 2026 syllabus, one thing became uncomfortably clear: we ar...
answer_text: As I worked on an AI policy for my Spring 2026 syllabus, one thing became uncomfortably clear: we are managing tomorrow’s classrooms with yesterday’s rules. Much of the current conversation about AI in education centers on regulation: what should be restricted, monitored, or prohibited. Yet this focus risks overlooking a more fundamental issue. Generative AI can now perform many of the tasks educators have traditionally used to measure learning, revealing the fragility of long-standing assumptions about assessment and academic rigor. Rather than treating AI primarily as a threat to academic integrity, this article argues that it should be understood as a catalyst for rethinking what meaningful learning entails in contemporary education. The pressing challenge is not whether students will use AI, but whether education systems are prepared to move beyond surface-level policies and redesign learning for an AI-rich world.

question_title: I was on CBS News Chicago yesterday as part of their "Eye on AI" series to talk about what we're see...
answer_text: I was on CBS News Chicago yesterday as part of their "Eye on AI" series to talk about what we're seeing in schools right now. Towards the end a Zoom notification made a guest appearance on my face for about ten seconds - proof that you can help people navigate emerging technology everyday and still get taken out by a pop-up.

question_title: Fortunately, it takes a lot more than that to keep me from talking about AI and education. We covere...
answer_text: Fortunately, it takes a lot more than that to keep me from talking about AI and education. We covered a lot of ground in a short segment, but three things stood out:

question_title: The first question was about how we're tackling AI adoption in schools. I shared that before we ever...
answer_text: The first question was about how we're tackling AI adoption in schools. I shared that before we ever get to students, we work with teachers and leaders to build confidence — not just with the tools, but with the ethica that l adoption of these tools. No one is an expert on AI in education yet. We're all learning together, and that means focusing on limitations just as much as opportunities.

question_title: We talked about one of the biggest misconceptions in education right now: that AI is only for cheati...
answer_text: We talked about one of the biggest misconceptions in education right now: that AI is only for cheating. Students are internalizing that message, and it's leading to two negative outcomes. Some are hiding their use, with no support or training, which can affect not just their schoolwork but their mental health and wellbeing. Others are avoiding AI entirely, even as they head into college and careers where these skills will matter. We need transparency and real discussions, not fear.

question_title: Finally, we got into how teachers are actually using AI. In our experience, intentional, AI-literate...
answer_text: Finally, we got into how teachers are actually using AI. In our experience, intentional, AI-literate use doesn't necessarily save teachers a ton of time. But it means teachers are upgrading instructional materials, supporting diverse learners, and getting back to the work they love most — building relationships and helping young people find a love of learning.

question_title: Overall it was a great segment and thanks to the CBS team who also shared AI for Education's new fre...
answer_text: Overall it was a great segment and thanks to the CBS team who also shared AI for Education's new free student AI literacy course and our guide for parents (links in the comments if you want to check them out).

question_title: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming the field of education by improving teaching methods, l...
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming the field of education by improving teaching methods, learning experiences, and administrative processes. AI refers to computer systems that can perform tasks that normally require human intelligence, such as learning, problem-solving, and decision-making. In education, AI technologies are being used to create more personalized and efficient learning environments.

question_title: One of the most significant advantages of AI in education is personalized learning. AI-powered platf...
answer_text: One of the most significant advantages of AI in education is personalized learning. AI-powered platforms can analyze students’ learning patterns, strengths, and weaknesses, and then provide customized lessons and exercises based on individual needs. This helps students learn at their own pace and improves their understanding of complex subjects.

question_title: AI also assists teachers by automating routine tasks such as grading assignments, tracking student p...
answer_text: AI also assists teachers by automating routine tasks such as grading assignments, tracking student progress, and managing administrative work. This allows teachers to focus more on teaching, mentoring, and interacting with students. Additionally, AI-based tutoring systems and chatbots can provide instant feedback and support to students anytime, making learning more accessible.

question_title: However, the use of AI in education also raises certain challenges, including concerns about data pr...
answer_text: However, the use of AI in education also raises certain challenges, including concerns about data privacy, dependence on technology, and the need for proper teacher training. Educational institutions must ensure that AI tools are used ethically and effectively.

question_title: In conclusion, AI has the potential to significantly enhance the quality of education by making lear...
answer_text: In conclusion, AI has the potential to significantly enhance the quality of education by making learning more personalized, efficient, and accessible. When implemented responsibly, it can support both teachers and students in achieving better educational outcomes.

question_title: This is as much a philosophical conundrum as it is a practical issue of academic integrity. My argum...
answer_text: This is as much a philosophical conundrum as it is a practical issue of academic integrity. My argument is that the sooner we interrogate our neo-liberal assumptions about individual exceptionalism and the primacy of the single human brain as the primary target of education, the more prepared we will be to confront this eventuality. I believe we should seriously consider and systematically integrate alternative and complementary perspectives of cognition, including distributed cognition, embodied cognition, and other "wide" perspectives that have rich theoretical and empirical histories in the cognitive sciences.

question_title: The rapid diffusion of artificial intelligence (AI) is reshaping labor markets, organizational desig...
answer_text: The rapid diffusion of artificial intelligence (AI) is reshaping labor markets, organizational design, and sources of economic advantage. While technological capital has received substantial attention, comparatively less emphasis has been placed on the human cognitive and mental capacities required to complement, govern, and productively deploy AI systems. This paper advances the concept of brain capital—defined as the combined stock of brain health and brain skills—as a foundational economic asset in the AI-era. Drawing on interdisciplinary evidence from neuroscience, economics, public health, education, and organizational science, the paper synthesizes current knowledge on the societal and economic returns of investing in brain capital across the life course.

question_title: If I was in college today and confused about my career choices because of AI, I would simply priorit...
answer_text: If I was in college today and confused about my career choices because of AI, I would simply prioritize knowledge over everything else. Knowledge never goes waste. Not even with AI. Looking back, when we were young, we did not have access to good books or teachers. There was no YouTube or internet where we could learn. Today anybody who wants to pursue any discipline, you have access to the best knowledge on your fingertips. You can directly hear what Nobel prize winners and field medal winners think about their own discipline or what best economists or artists think. So drink it! AI can’t defeat a curious and prepared mind.

question_title: When education is limited to simplified, single-path explanations, exposure to representational dive...
answer_text: When education is limited to simplified, single-path explanations, exposure to representational diversity decreases. The brain may not be challenged to abstract, generalize, or reinterpret concepts across contexts. In that sense, we may be training human minds with insufficient cognitive variety. This leaves us with a critical question: 𝐀𝐫𝐞 𝐰𝐞 𝐨𝐩𝐭𝐢𝐦𝐢𝐳𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐞𝐝 𝐨𝐟 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐥𝐞𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐰𝐡𝐢𝐥𝐞 𝐬𝐚𝐜𝐫𝐢𝐟𝐢𝐜𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐝𝐞𝐩𝐭𝐡 𝐨𝐟 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐡𝐞𝐧𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧?

question_title: Education should not merely aim for universal comprehension. It should aim to help each child grow a...
answer_text: Education should not merely aim for universal comprehension. It should aim to help each child grow at the edge of their ability. The real question is 𝐰𝐡𝐞𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫 𝐜𝐮𝐫𝐫𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐭𝐫𝐞𝐧𝐝𝐬 𝐚𝐫𝐞 𝐦𝐨𝐯𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐮𝐬 𝐭𝐨𝐰𝐚𝐫𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐞𝐝𝐠𝐞 𝐨𝐫 𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐥𝐲 𝐬𝐦𝐨𝐨𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐢𝐭 𝐨𝐮𝐭.

answer_text: Recently I shared a short video across my social channels with a simple message about learning and the role of AI. Has AI started to feel less like a tool and more like an addiction for some learners?

question_title: This week shows a clear pattern: AI is transitioning from a technology novelty toward structured pol...
answer_text: This week shows a clear pattern: AI is transitioning from a technology novelty toward structured policy, curriculum planning, and ethical engagement. Leaders — from national summits to district-level policies — are increasingly framing AI as an enhancement of education rather than a replacement for human educators. Yet, as research points out, unexamined adoption can undermine learner agency and critical thinking. The central question for 2026 remains: How do we harness AI’s power while safeguarding human agency and pedagogical integrity?

question_title: This pilot study addresses the challenge of passive learning and AI-dependency in vocational IT educ...
answer_text: This pilot study addresses the challenge of passive learning and AI-dependency in vocational IT education. It evaluates a socio-technical intervention—a behavioral "Carrot and Stick" approach—integrated with a custom AI-NLP assessment platform for 70 students. The "Carrot" rewards iterative process-based effort (60% of the grade), while the "Stick" utilizes Sentence-BERT to enforce academic integrity and classroom discipline.

question_title: Results from a 16-week quasi-experimental study show the intervention group significantly outperform...
answer_text: Results from a 16-week quasi-experimental study show the intervention group significantly outperformed the traditional control group ($M=83.66$ vs. $M=51.00$). A Random Forest classifier achieved 93% accuracy in predicting success, identifying social factors like peer support and teacher engagement as the strongest drivers of student transformation. Conversely, purely technical AI metrics showed lower predictive value. The findings conclude that while AI provides essential functional infrastructure, human scaffolding and clear behavioral boundaries remain the primary catalysts for moving students from passive consumption to active engagement.

question_title: 🧑‍🏫 Path 1: The Instructional Design Lens of Google Deepmind (see their Future of Learning paper) ou...
answer_text: 🧑‍🏫 Path 1: The Instructional Design Lens of Google Deepmind (see their Future of Learning paper) outlines an architecture grounded in five core pedagogical principles, like managing cognitive load and inspiring active learning. Their framework asks: What should the AI do to act like an expert tutor?

question_title: 🧠 Path 2: The Cognitive Science Lens that OpenAI is embracing after working with the Stanford Accele...
answer_text: 🧠 Path 2: The Cognitive Science Lens that OpenAI is embracing after working with the Stanford Accelerator for Learning leans heavily into cognitive science. They map AI interactions to biological mechanisms like task persistence, metacognitive self-monitoring, and schema construction. Their framework asks: What should happen in the learner's brain?

question_title: What's also worth noting: these paths diverge to some degree but they can also reinforce each other ...
answer_text: What's also worth noting: these paths diverge to some degree but they can also reinforce each other in more expansive learning environments. For instance, I'm really excited by the work of Edvisor.ai, which is building just that: adaptive higher ed course packs, including AI-resistance assessments, that deploy gold standard teaching frameworks and inspire productive learner behaviors at the same time.

question_title: After all, the goal from my perspective isn't to "prompt" the AI to act like a teacher. It's to buil...
answer_text: After all, the goal from my perspective isn't to "prompt" the AI to act like a teacher. It's to build frameworks that allow students, for instance, to learn in different modalities (i.e. dual encoding) while preserving certain behaviors (like productive struggle and retrieval practice) that are key to maintaining capacities of critical thinking, judgement, and creativity.

question_title: We've seen the prototype in Khan Academy — adaptive, self-paced, on demand. Now extend it: a virtual...
answer_text: We've seen the prototype in Khan Academy — adaptive, self-paced, on demand. Now extend it: a virtual classroom where instruction is tailored to each student's pace, learning style, and demonstrated gaps in real time. The "teacher" is tireless, infinitely patient, and has access to the sum of human knowledge. The environment carries full academic, behavioral, and cyber awareness with no blind spots.

question_title: “I’ve noticed a fascinating phenomenon in my thirty years of teaching: schools and schooling are inc...
answer_text: “I’ve noticed a fascinating phenomenon in my thirty years of teaching: schools and schooling are increasingly irrelevant to the great enterprises of the planet. No one believes anymore that scientists are trained in science classes or politicians in civics classes or poets in English classes. The truth is that schools don’t really teach anything except how to obey orders. This is a great mystery to me because thousands of humane, caring people work in schools as teachers and aides and administrators, but the abstract logic of the institution overwhelms their individual contributions. Although teachers to care and do work very, very hard, the institution is psychopathic — it has no conscience. It rings a bell and the young man in the middle of writing a poem must close his notebook and move to a different cell where he must memorize that humans and monkeys derive from a common ancestor.” John Taylor Gatto

question_title: I have a question about AI literacy and regulation in education that I have been thinking about rece...
answer_text: I have a question about AI literacy and regulation in education that I have been thinking about recently in relation to Byung-Chul Han's insightful and provocative analyses on the "achievement society."

question_title: The question is what happens if you mix the logic of achievement and self-exploitation with AI in ed...
answer_text: The question is what happens if you mix the logic of achievement and self-exploitation with AI in education. In the capability-expansion model of AI literacy, AI is viewed as a technology that augments human agency. Uncontrolled expansion of agency, in Han's terms, is actually a highway to hell.

question_title: "Assuming that Han’s analysis is right, this begs the question what happens when students and teache...
answer_text: "Assuming that Han’s analysis is right, this begs the question what happens when students and teachers start to use AI. If the underlying logic of self-achievement is not regulated, it will potentially just accelerate the speed that students career toward burnout. If teachers are assumed to become “AI literate” so that they can teach with and about AI, this fast-moving technology will create an endless need for self-improvement. No budget for continuous professional development will be enough."

answer_text: Given the greater need for contextual flexibility rather than highly specialised content and pedagogy in K-12, fine-tuning and RAG approaches are often unnecessarily restrictive vs the flexible use of out-of-the-box LLMs contextualised progressively through layered prompting (i.e. at system, admin and teacher levels).

question_title: First we should avoid pejoratively judging “wrappers” (since almost every AIEd tool is one) but rath...
answer_text: First we should avoid pejoratively judging “wrappers” (since almost every AIEd tool is one) but rather begin evaluating the quality and “thickness” of these wrappers by the KPIs they seek to achieve, ie whether they are reliable and valid measures of holistic and long-term LEARNING (vs usage/”engagement”).

question_title: Second, we should understand that any learning experience is determined not just by the quality of t...
answer_text: Second, we should understand that any learning experience is determined not just by the quality of the LLM response but also by the way the response is delivered.

question_title: UI also determines frequency of teacher oversight and quality of LLM responses (ie bite-sizing learn...
answer_text: UI also determines frequency of teacher oversight and quality of LLM responses (ie bite-sizing learning tasks to improve accuracy and prompt alignment).

question_title: I’ve been thinking a lot about how we prepare the next generation for a world that’s being reshaped ...
answer_text: I’ve been thinking a lot about how we prepare the next generation for a world that’s being reshaped by AI. And honestly, it’s time we treated AI education with the same urgency we do with subjects like math or science. Just as a working knowledge of the Microsoft Office suite is now a baseline for many jobs, understanding AI tools and their implications will soon be a must-have skill — not just for tech roles, but for nearly every profession.

question_title: The problem isn’t just about teaching students how to use AI. It’s about teaching them how to think ...
answer_text: The problem isn’t just about teaching students how to use AI. It’s about teaching them how to think about it. Ethical considerations — like taking unjustified shortcuts, copyright issues, creating essays without knowing the subject matter, among others — are just as important as the technical skills. If we don’t equip students with the ability to question AI’s role in society, we risk creating a generation that uses these tools without understanding the consequences.

question_title: There are challenges, of course. Many schools still lack the resources or training to integrate AI e...
answer_text: There are challenges, of course. Many schools still lack the resources or training to integrate AI education effectively. And there’s a real risk of focusing too much on the technical side while neglecting the broader implications. But this isn’t just about preparing students for the future — it’s about preparing them for the present. AI is already here, and it’s changing the way we work, communicate, and even think.

question_title: We need to start now. Let’s make sure every student has the knowledge and critical thinking skills t...
answer_text: We need to start now. Let’s make sure every student has the knowledge and critical thinking skills to navigate an AI-driven world — not just as users, but as responsible creators and decision-makers.

question_title: The AI started gently, introducing itself with a calm, friendly voice. Instead of dictating notes, i...
answer_text: The AI started gently, introducing itself with a calm, friendly voice. The students felt their usual anxiety ease, and a quiet excitement filled the room. Learning suddenly felt different—more like a conversation than a lecture.

question_title: As the day went on, the AI noticed which students were struggling and offered hints and examples tai...
answer_text: As the day went on, the AI noticed which students were struggling and offered hints and examples tailored to each one. When a shy student raised their hand, the AI responded with patience and encouragement, making the classroom feel safe and inclusive.

question_title: Are educators ready to embrace AI, or will they be left behind? It's a question many are asking as s...
answer_text: Are educators ready to embrace AI, or will they be left behind? It's a question many are asking as students increasingly leverage tools like ChatGPT and Gemini. Instead of fearing obsolescence, one educator decided to experiment — tasking AI with building a language unit, complete with rubrics and student guides. The outcome wasn't about replacement, but rather augmentation. By refining AI-generated content, the teacher was able to personalize the unit to meet the diverse needs and engagement styles of the students.

question_title: This approach underscores a vital lesson: AI can provide structure and save time, but it requires a ...
answer_text: This approach underscores a vital lesson: AI can provide structure and save time, but it requires a human touch to foster creativity and connection. By allowing AI to handle the scaffolding, the teacher was able to step back, coach, and focus on what truly matters. It was an honor to witness students expand their poems, sketch posters, and offer constructive feedback to one another. Curious to know how other educators are integrating AI into their teaching practices? Share your experiences! #AIinEducation #EdTech #FutureofTeaching #InnovationInEducation #ChatGPTinEducation

question_title: The use of ChatGPT and other LLMs is ubiquitous, It’s hard to imagine a world today where students w...
answer_text: The use of ChatGPT and other LLMs is ubiquitous, It’s hard to imagine a world today where students who can leverage off it will simply turn their back on it. While concerns about cheating persist, it is imperative to shift the focus from the method to the outcome of learning. To this end, educational testing methods must also evolve to better reflect the skills needed in the modern workplace.
